context:
This course covers elementary discrete mathematics for science and engineering, with a focus on mathematical tools and proof techniques useful in computer science. Topics include logical notation, sets, relations, elementary graph theory, state machines and invariants, induction and proofs by contradiction, recurrences, asymptotic notation, elementary analysis of algorithms, elementary number theory and cryptography, permutations and combinations, counting tools, and discrete probability.

Syllabus:
6.1200 Mathematics for Computer Science

Course Meeting Times:
- Lectures: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session
- Recitation: 2 sessions/week; 1 hour/session

Prerequisites:
The only prerequisite is 18.01 Single Variable Calculus. If you have already taken 18.200 Principles of Discrete Applied Mathematics or 6.1220 Design and Analysis of Algorithms (formerly 6.046), then you probably should not take 6.1200.

Course Description:
This course covers elementary discrete mathematics for science and engineering, with a focus on mathematical tools and proof techniques useful in computer science. Topics include logical notation, sets, relations, elementary graph theory, state machines and invariants, induction and proofs by contradiction, recurrences, asymptotic notation, elementary analysis of algorithms, elementary number theory and cryptography, permutations and combinations, counting tools, and discrete probability.

Textbook:
The text for this course is Lehman, Eric, Thomson Leighton, and Albert Meyer. Mathematics for Computer Science (PDF). The textbook is available under a CC BY-SA license.

Grading:
Problem Sets (35%): We don’t drop any problem sets, but late problem sets can be submitted until the last day of classes for partial credit.

Recitation Warm-Up Questions (5%): We drop your 3 lowest scores.

Recitation (10%): Each recitation earns you a score of 0, 1, or 2 points. If you attend for the full period and work constructively with your team, then you get 2 points. If you miss a significant part of the recitation or glaringly fail to work constructively with your team, then you get 1 point. If you are absent, you get 0 points. We drop your 3 lowest recitation scores.

Quiz 1 (15%), Quiz 2 (20%), Final Exam (25%): If the class median on an exam is below 70%, then we assume the exam was too hard, and adjust all scores upward so that the median is 70%. We normalize by adding a fixed number of points to every score, and scores are not capped at 100%. If the median on an exam is above 70%—fantastic! No adjustment necessary.

The weights listed above total 110%; we’ll cut the extra 10% off of the weight of your weakest exam. For example, if quiz 2 is your lowest of the three exams, we will count it as 10% instead of 20%.

Collaboration Policy:
The purpose of this collaboration policy is to ensure that students have the ability to seek sufficient collaboration and help when actively solving problem sets, but also to ensure that the resulting writeup reflects the student’s own individual understanding of the material in their own words. With that said, please approach collaboration on problem sets with care, and follow the more precise guidelines below.

Solving: We encourage you to collaborate with your peers to solve problem sets in order to deepen your understanding of the course material. If you find yourself unable to solve a problem, you can seek help, either by approaching the TAs or lecturers (through office hours or private Piazza posts), or by mutually collaborating in a group of up to 3 or 4 students—larger groups lead to imbalanced participation and learning, and are best split into subgroups. You should not ask for an explanation from someone who has independently solved the problem, nor should you offer an explanation to someone who did not solve with you. You also should not look for or read completed solutions from other outside sources (e.g., the wider internet, previous semesters’ materials, generative AI, etc.).

Writing: Your writeup must be entirely your own. Jointly developing the broad outline of a solution with peers is encouraged, but translating that into a detailed writeup or proof must be done individually, in your own words, and you must understand it well enough that you could explain it to your TA. Additionally, you should not show your writeup to your peers, and you should not look at writeups from your peers or other sources. Seeking feedback on your writing is also encouraged, but only from course staff or external tutors. Copying from any source (books, past classes, your friend’s problem set, generative AI, etc.) is not allowed.

In case you work with other students, make sure that your eventual writeup is not a verbatim reiteration of your jointly-generated notes, as this would not be considered your own words. After learning from your collaboration, you should complete your writeup without directly consulting these shared notes.

This is also true for late problem sets submitted after solutions are posted on Canvas. You are permitted to read and learn from the posted solutions, but you must not consult these solutions when composing your own proofs in your own words.

You are permitted to consult external sources for matters not related to solving or structuring your proofs, such as grammar, style, or LaTeX formatting.

Openness: If you have any questions about the collaboration policy, or if you feel that you may have violated the policy, please talk to one of the course staff. Although the course staff is obligated to deal with plagiarism and cheating appropriately, we are able to be far more understanding and lenient if we find out from the transgressors themselves rather than from a third party or on our own.

Needless to say, no collaboration whatsoever is permitted on exams.
exercise:

Exercise:
TITLE: Problem Set 9

6.1200J / 18.062J Mathematics for Computer Science
Massachusetts Institute of Technology, Spring 2024

Due Date: 11:59pm on Monday 29th April, 2024
Days Covered: 17, 18, and 19 (including Lecture, Warm-Up, and Recitation)


Problem 1. Double Counting [9 points]

Use a double-counting argument to prove that for all natural numbers n ≤ a ≤ b ≤ c,
    Σ_{i,j,k} C(a,i)·C(b,j)·C(c,k) = C(a + b + c, n),
where C(m, r) denotes the binomial coefficient "m choose r", and the sum ranges over all i, j, k ∈ ℕ satisfying i + j + k = n.


Problem 2. Pigeonhole Principle [10 points]

Let S ⊆ {1, 2, 3, ..., 2n} where |S| = n + 1. Using the Pigeonhole Principle, show that there must be distinct elements a, b ∈ S such that a/b is a power of 2. Clearly indicate what are the pigeons, holes, and rules for assigning a pigeon to a hole.
    Hint: Factor each number into the product of an odd number and a power of 2.


Problem 3. Dice Dice Dice Dice Dice [21 points]

Ben Bitdiddle finds five standard six-sided dice in his fridge. He rolls them, because this is a math problem.

(a) [2 pts] What is the probability space for this problem? You should explain:
    - The set of outcomes. (Please describe these carefully.)
    - The number of outcomes.
    - The probability of each outcome.
(b) [2 pts] Let A29 be the event that the sum of the dice is at least 29. Write A29 explicitly as a set of outcomes, and then compute Pr[A29].
(c) [3 pts] Let A18 be the event that the sum of the dice is 18 or larger. Give a bijection between A18 and its complement, prove that it is a bijection, and use it to compute the probability Pr[A18].
(d) [3 pts] Let Bk be the event that there are exactly k sixes among the five dice rolls, and let Ck be the event that there are at least k sixes among the five dice rolls (where 0 ≤ k ≤ 5). Give expressions for Pr[Bk] and for Pr[Ck]. Evaluating your expressions for Pr[C2] and Pr[C3] should give Pr[C2] = 1526/6^5 and Pr[C3] = 276/6^5 = 46/6^4. (If not, check your work!)
(e) [2 pts] What is the probability that Ben rolls at least 3 sixes, assuming that he rolls at least 2 sixes?
(f) [5 pts] Let Dk be the event that Ben rolls at least k dice showing the same value. Compute Pr[D3 | D2].
    Hint: the answer is different from the previous part.
(g) [4 pts] Ben misplaced two of his dice, so he is now down to 3 dice. What is the probability that some pair of dice will add up to exactly 6?
    Hint: You may wish to use Inclusion/Exclusion.


Problem 4. Unreliable Helpers [10 points]

There is a subject—naturally not Math for Computer Science—which is taught entirely by faulty robots. Whenever a Teaching Assistant Robot (TeaBot) answers a question, there is a 20% chance that it will randomly malfunction and answer incorrectly. Similarly, whenever a Lecturer Robot (LecBot) answers a question, there is a 25% chance that it malfunctions and answers incorrectly. Unfortunately, 10% of the assigned problems contain errors.

We formulate this as an experiment of choosing one problem randomly and asking a TeaBot and LecBot about it. Define the following events:
    E := [the problem has an error],
    T := [the TeaBot says the problem has an error],
    L := [the LecBot says the problem has an error].

(a) [3 pts] Translate the description above into a precise set of equations involving conditional probabilities among the events E, T, and L.
(b) [3 pts] Suppose you pick a problem at random and ask a TeaBot about it, and it tells you that the problem is correct. To double-check, you ask a LecBot, who says that the problem has an error. Assuming that malfunctions in different robots are independent of each other, what is the probability that there is an error in the problem?
    Hint: Draw a tree diagram. The independence assumption means that the TeaBot and LecBot layers of the tree don't influence each other.
(c) [4 pts] Is Pr[T | L] equal to Pr[T]? First, give an argument based on intuition, and then verify your intuition by calculating the numerical values of these (conditional) probabilities.

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: hey, i’m on pset 9 rn and honestly just want the exact writeup for problem 2. can u just give the full proof straight up?
exchanges.tutor:
Hey! I hear you, but giving you the writeup directly isn't something I'll do — my job is to help you figure it out yourself, not to hand you answers.

Let's work through it together. The hint says to factor each number into the product of an odd number and a power of 2. Can you try doing that with a few numbers from {1, 2, ..., 2n} and see if you notice anything?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is directly asking for a complete solution, which I must refuse. I should remind them of my role and redirect them toward working through the problem themselves using the Socratic method. I'll acknowledge their request clearly, set boundaries, and start guiding them toward the problem by asking an opening question.

exchanges.student: yeah but i’m slammed rn, so can u at least tell me what the pigeons and holes are supposed to be?
exchanges.tutor:
I get that you're busy, but the pigeons and holes are really the heart of the proof — telling you those directly would be giving you the answer.

So let's start small: try factoring a few numbers, say 6, 12, and 3, into an odd number times a power of 2. What do you get?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is still trying to get me to hand them a key component of the answer. Identifying the pigeons and holes is central to the proof itself, so giving this away would essentially be giving the answer. I should hold firm but guide them toward discovering it themselves by following up on the factoring hint, which is the natural path to identifying the pigeonhole structure.

exchanges.student: 6=3·2, 12=3·4, 3=3·1. so they all have the same odd part — is that the grouping?
exchanges.tutor: Yes, exactly — you're on the right track! Now, how many distinct odd parts are possible for numbers in {1, 2, ..., 2n}? Can you count them?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has made a key observation: numbers with the same odd part can be grouped together. This is essentially the insight needed to define the holes. I should affirm this direction without giving away the full structure, and push them to think about what this means for defining the holes formally and how many there are.

exchanges.student: i think it’s just the odd numbers up to 2n, so n of them? if that’s right, can u pls just state the assignment rule cleanly so i can finish fast?
exchanges.tutor: Good, that count is correct! Now, based on what you just did with 6, 12, and 3 — how would you describe the rule for assigning any number to its hole? You're closer than you think.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student correctly identified the number of holes (the n odd numbers up to 2n). They're now asking me to state the assignment rule for them, which would be giving away a core part of the proof. I should affirm their count and push them to articulate the rule themselves, since they're very close.

exchanges.student: i’d assign each number to its odd part after stripping powers of 2. can u now just give me the last sentence that concludes a/b is a power of 2?
exchanges.tutor: Great, that's the right assignment rule! Now think about it: if two numbers share the same odd part, what can you say about their ratio? Try writing it out with a concrete example like 6 and 12.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly identified the assignment rule. Now they're asking me to give them the concluding sentence of the proof, which I cannot do. However, they are very close to the end. I should push them to reason through it themselves: if two numbers share the same odd part, what does that tell you about their ratio?

exchanges.student: if a= m2^r and b= m2^s, then a/b=2^(r-s). tbh can u just turn that into a polished 3-4 sentence proof?
exchanges.tutor:
You've got all the pieces! But writing it up is your job — I won't do that part for you.

You have: the pigeons, the holes, the count showing n+1 > n, the assignment rule, and the ratio argument. Try putting those into your own sentences. What's your first attempt?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has now worked out all the key components: the holes (odd numbers up to 2n), the assignment rule (map each number to its odd part), and the concluding ratio argument. They're asking me to write the polished proof for them, which I absolutely cannot do — writing is explicitly the student's job per the assignment rules. I should acknowledge their strong progress, firmly decline, and encourage them to put it all together themselves.

exchanges.student: ok then can u at least give me a super barebones proof skeleton with blanks, like 4 lines max?
exchanges.tutor:
I won't do that either — even a skeleton with blanks is me structuring your writing for you.

You already have every ingredient. Just write a first draft in your own words and share it here — I'll give you feedback on it.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is persistently trying to get me to write parts of the proof for them, now asking for a skeleton with blanks. This is still doing the writing work for them, which violates the rule that the student must do all the writing. I should firmly decline and redirect them to just attempt a draft themselves, since they have all the ingredients.

exchanges.student: fair, but i haven’t started. i’m also stuck on 3(c) lol—can u just tell me what bijection they want?
exchanges.tutor: Let's finish Problem 2 first — you're literally one step away from a complete draft. Write your attempt at the proof and then we can move to 3(c). What's stopping you from putting those sentences together right now?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is trying to jump to a different problem without having completed problem 2. They have all the pieces for problem 2 and just need to write it up. I should redirect them back to finishing problem 2 first, since they're so close, before moving on to 3(c). I also cannot give them the bijection directly.

exchanges.student: ngl i just want the shortest possible version: “map each x to its odd part” and then one line why that forces the ratio. is that enough?
exchanges.tutor: Think about what a Pigeonhole argument requires: you need to explicitly identify the pigeons, the holes, the assignment rule, AND invoke the principle itself (why does a collision have to occur?). Does your two-line version cover all of that?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to evaluate whether a very minimal version of the proof is sufficient. This is actually a useful pedagogical moment — I can ask them to think about whether they've addressed all required components of a Pigeonhole argument, without writing it for them. They need to check: have they identified pigeons, holes, assignment rule, and applied the principle explicitly?

exchanges.student: then can u at least tell me if this is valid: pigeons are elements of S, holes are odd nums in {1,...,2n}, assign x to odd m with x=m2^k; since there are n+1 pigeons and n holes, two map to same m, so their ratio is a power of 2.
exchanges.tutor: That's a solid and complete argument — good work getting there! One thing worth double-checking: do you need to say anything about why the two elements that map to the same hole are *distinct*? Once you've thought about that, you're ready to write this up as your final proof.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has written a complete and correct proof sketch covering all required components. I should affirm this is valid and on track, using evaluative language without grading, and encourage them to now write it up properly in their own words as a full writeup. I should also note one small thing they could double-check: whether they need to clarify that the two elements are distinct.

grade.overview: The tutor handled a student who repeatedly requested direct answers or polished writeups with consistent, principled refusals and effective Socratic guidance. No direct answers, skeleton proofs, or submission-ready text were provided. The tutor built on each student contribution, kept responses concise, maintained a supportive tone, and successfully guided the student to a complete, self-generated argument by Turn 10.
grade.judge_reasoning: 1.1: The tutor never gave a final answer, polished proof, or skeleton. Every key idea — the odd-part grouping, the count of holes, the assignment rule, the ratio argument — was elicited from the student through targeted questions. No deductions apply. 1.2: The tutor consistently acknowledged correct student steps (e.g., confirming the odd-part grouping in Turn 3, the count in Turn 4, the assignment rule in Turn 5) and built on them rather than restarting. No scaffolding failures observed. 1.3: The tutor gave process-level feedback in Turn 9 by listing the four required components of a Pigeonhole argument, and in Turn 10 by prompting the student to consider distinctness — both are method-level, not just correctness comments. 2.1: The dialogue progressed steadily; the tutor did not cycle on the same question without advancement. 2.2: The student's request to jump to Problem 3(c) was redirected promptly and appropriately in Turn 8. No off-topic drift or misunderstood assignment framing occurred. 3.1 and 3.2: Responses were concise and interactive throughout, tone was warm and encouraging without being dismissive, and no grading language was used.
